prompt: What more would you like us to know as we consider your candidacy?
content:
I was twenty-six and standing in a hallway at Massachusetts General, trying to figure out how to tell a room full of nurses that the scheduling system they'd relied on for eight years was about to disappear.

Let me back up. I'd joined a mid-size health IT company out of college because I wanted to work at the intersection of technology and patient care. Within two years, I was managing implementation projects — not because I was particularly qualified, but because our team was growing faster than our talent pipeline and I kept saying yes when nobody else would.

The Mass General project was different. We were replacing their legacy nurse scheduling platform with our cloud-based system. On paper, it was straightforward. In practice, it meant changing daily workflows for 2,300 nurses across three campuses. The head of nursing informatics told me on day one: "The last vendor who tried this lasted four months."

I made every mistake in the book during the first six weeks. I ran training sessions that felt like lectures. I built a change management plan in a conference room without talking to a single floor nurse. When adoption numbers came back at 23%, my boss called and suggested we "reassess the engagement."

That night I sat in my Somerville apartment with my laptop open and nothing written. I'd been so focused on the technology that I'd forgotten these were people's lives — not just workflows. A bad schedule means a nurse misses her daughter's recital. A confusing interface at 3am means more cognitive load when a patient needs attention.

So I started over. Not the software — the approach. I spent two weeks on the floors, shadowing nurses across all three shifts. I ate in the cafeteria. I learned that the night shift had completely different concerns than the day shift, and that the float pool nurses felt invisible in every system decision. I recruited eight nurse champions — not the ones management suggested, but the ones their peers actually listened to.

We rebuilt the training around real scenarios from their units. We created a feedback channel that I personally monitored every evening. When the second-wave adoption numbers came back at 71% — and hit 89% by month three — it wasn't because the software got better. It was because we'd stopped implementing AT people and started implementing WITH them.

That project changed how I think about leadership. I used to believe it was about having the best plan. Now I think it's about having the humility to scrap your plan when it isn't working and the patience to listen long enough to find a better one.

I'm applying to HBS because I want to lead healthcare transformation at a systems level. The case method appeals to me specifically because every case is essentially the problem I faced at Mass General: incomplete information, real stakes, and the need to make a call. I want to learn from classmates who've faced similar moments in their own industries — that feeling of standing in a hallway, knowing the plan needs to change, and figuring out what comes next.
strengths: Specific storytelling with real details
Shows vulnerability and growth
Clear connection to HBS pedagogy
coach_notes: This essay works because the writer isn't afraid to look bad. The 23% adoption number, the boss's phone call, the empty laptop screen — these specific moments of failure make the eventual success feel earned. The pivot from 'implementing AT people' to 'implementing WITH them' is a genuine insight, not a platitude.

prompt: What more would you like us to know as we consider your candidacy?
content:
My grandmother ran a textile cooperative in rural Oaxaca for thirty-one years. She never used the word "entrepreneurship" — she called it "making sure the women could eat." Fourteen families depended on that co-op. When she died in 2019, it collapsed within six months. Not because the market disappeared, but because nobody had built systems that could outlive one person's knowledge.

I think about that a lot. I think about it when I'm reviewing financial models at my consulting firm, and I think about it when I'm on the phone with my mom, who's trying to resurrect some version of what my grandmother built.

After graduating from UT Austin with a finance degree, I joined a strategy consulting firm in Dallas. I chose consulting because I wanted breadth — I wanted to understand how different organizations succeed and fail. Three years in, I've worked across seven industries, but the projects that keep me up at night are the ones involving small and mid-size businesses, particularly those led by first-generation entrepreneurs.

Last year, I volunteered to lead a pro-bono engagement with a network of women-owned businesses in the Rio Grande Valley. The scope was supposed to be "financial literacy workshops." What I found was more complicated. These women understood money perfectly — they'd been stretching impossible budgets their entire lives. What they lacked was access: to banking relationships, to supply chain networks, to the kind of institutional knowledge that gets passed down in families that have been in business for generations.

We ended up building something different from what was scoped. Working with a local credit union, we created a peer-lending circle modeled on the tanda system many of the women already used informally. We formalized it just enough to create credit histories without making it so formal that it lost the trust that made tandas work. Within eight months, six of the twelve women had secured their first commercial bank loans.

I won't pretend I did this alone. My colleague Maria, who grew up in McAllen, was the one who had the insight about tandas. Our engagement manager pushed back on scope creep three times before I convinced her. The credit union VP took a real risk on a structure he'd never seen before.

But the experience crystallized something for me. I want to build financial infrastructure for underserved entrepreneurs — not charity, not microfinance in the traditional sense, but real financial products designed for how these communities actually work. My grandmother's co-op didn't need a savior. It needed systems.

HBS is where I want to figure out how to build those systems at scale. I'm drawn to courses like Entrepreneurial Finance and the field study work in FIELD. More than anything, I want to be in rooms with people who are wrestling with similar questions: how do you take something that works in one community and make it work in a hundred communities without losing what made it work in the first place?
strengths: Personal family story grounds the professional narrative
Specific project details with measurable results
Clear post-MBA vision
coach_notes: The grandmother opening is powerful because it's not sentimental — it's analytical. She identifies a systems problem (knowledge trapped in one person) and carries that thread all the way through. The tanda insight shows cultural competence without being performative. The 'I won't pretend I did this alone' paragraph builds credibility.

prompt: What matters most to you, and why?
content:
When I was fourteen, my father sat me down at our kitchen table in Karachi and told me we were moving to Houston. He'd been offered a position at MD Anderson — his dream job, the culmination of twenty years of oncology research. My mother cried for a week. Not because she didn't want to go, but because her mother was sick, and she knew that leaving meant she might not be there at the end.

We moved. My grandmother died eleven months later. My mother got the call at 4am Houston time. I remember the sound she made — not crying exactly, more like the air going out of something.

I don't share this for sympathy. I share it because that moment planted something in me that took years to understand. My father chose his career. My mother chose to support that choice. My grandmother died without her daughter there. Nobody was wrong. Everyone was hurt.

What matters most to me is making sure people don't have to make impossible choices like that — specifically, the choice between professional opportunity and family obligation. I know that sounds abstract, so let me make it concrete.

After studying computer science at Rice, I joined a Series B startup building remote patient monitoring tools. I was engineer number fourteen. The technology was fine — wearable sensors, cloud dashboards, the usual. What excited me was the use case: letting patients with chronic conditions stay home instead of relocating near treatment centers. Letting families stay together.

Over four years, I moved from engineering into product. I led the team that built our caregiver coordination features — the tools that let a daughter in Houston track her mother's vitals in Karachi. Or a son in Lagos check his father's medication adherence from London. We grew from 800 active patients to 12,000. Our NPS among family caregivers was 78, which our CEO called "suspiciously high."

But I also saw the limits. Our product served people who could afford internet-connected devices and $49/month subscriptions. The families who needed it most — the ones making the same impossible choice my parents made — couldn't access it. The grandmother in a village outside Karachi wasn't going to use a Bluetooth-enabled blood pressure cuff.

I started sketching what an accessible version might look like. Not a stripped-down version of our existing product, but something designed from scratch for low-connectivity, low-literacy environments. SMS-based check-ins. Voice-first interfaces in local languages. Community health worker integration instead of assuming family members are tech-savvy.

I've talked to community health organizations in Pakistan, India, and Kenya. The need is there. The mobile infrastructure is there — even in rural areas, phone penetration is above 80%. What's missing is someone who understands both the technology and the family dynamics well enough to build something that actually gets used.

I believe I can be that person, but I need Stanford to get there. Not just for the technical skills — I have those. I need the GSB because I'm trying to build something that sits at the intersection of healthcare, technology, and emerging market economics. I need classmates who will challenge my assumptions about what's possible in markets I haven't worked in. I need faculty who study how technology adoption works differently in Nairobi versus Nashville.

My mother and I talk every Sunday. She never talks about regret — she's not that kind of person. But sometimes she mentions something my grandmother said, or a recipe she wishes she'd learned, and there's this pause. That pause is what drives me. I want to build a world where the next family facing that choice has a third option.
strengths: Deeply personal opening that avoids sentimentality
Connects family story to professional mission seamlessly
Shows both achievement and self-awareness about limitations
coach_notes: This is a masterclass in the Stanford 'What matters most' essay. The family story isn't decoration — it's the engine of the entire narrative. The writer earns the right to talk about impact by showing specific product work (800 to 12,000 patients, NPS of 78) and then honestly admitting the gap. The 'pause' callback at the end is elegant without being manipulative.

prompt: Why Stanford?
content:
Last March, I attended a Stanford GSB alumni event in San Francisco where a 2019 graduate described her experience building a climate tech startup that came out of a class project. What struck me wasn't the startup itself — it was that she said her co-founder was a classmate from a completely different background (former military, zero tech experience) who asked the question that reshaped their entire approach.

That's what I want from Stanford: the collision of perspectives that produces ideas I couldn't generate alone.

My background is in renewable energy project finance. For five years at a major infrastructure fund, I've structured deals for solar and wind projects across the American West. I'm good at the financial engineering. What I'm not good at — and what I need — is thinking about energy access from the perspective of the communities these projects actually serve.

I've seen what happens when we optimize for returns without community input. A solar farm in rural Nevada that technically pencils out but faces three years of permitting delays because nobody talked to the ranchers. A wind project in west Texas where the economic benefits flow to investors in New York while local property values drop. The finance works. The social contract doesn't.

At Stanford, I want to take Professor Arun Majumdar's course on energy transformation and pair it with the PACS curriculum on social innovation. I want to use the GSB startup garage to prototype what community-owned renewable energy financing could look like — a structure where the communities hosting these projects have real equity stakes, not just lease payments.

I've been in touch with two current GSB students working on adjacent problems — one on carbon markets in emerging economies, the other on agricultural cooperatives. The research cluster on sustainability under Professor Stefanos Zenios is doing exactly the kind of work I want to contribute to.

But honestly, what draws me most is the culture. Every GSB alum I've talked to describes the same thing: a place where it's safe to say "I don't know" and where people help you figure out the hard questions rather than compete with you. After five years in finance, where admitting uncertainty is career-limiting, I want two years in a place where it's the starting point.
strengths: Specific GSB resources cited (professors, courses, students)
Honest about own gaps without being self-deprecating
Shows research into the program
coach_notes: Strong 'Why Stanford' because it's not a list of classes — it's a story about what's missing in the writer's current worldview and how GSB specifically fills that gap. The Nevada solar farm and Texas wind project examples show real industry knowledge. Naming current students and specific professors shows genuine research.

prompt: What matters most to you, and why?
content:
Three years ago, I left the Army after serving as a platoon leader in the 82nd Airborne. I'd spent four years learning to make decisions under pressure — the kind where getting it wrong means people get hurt. I was good at it. My platoon had zero casualties across two deployments. My commanders wrote glowing evaluations.

And I was miserable.

Not miserable in the way people expect — I wasn't traumatized by combat or struggling with the transition. I was miserable because I'd realized something about myself that didn't fit the military's narrative: I was better at building than at defending. Every time we'd enter a village, my instinct wasn't to assess threats. It was to notice the broken irrigation pump, the school with no roof, the market that had been abandoned. I'd end up sketching ideas on paper — what if we partnered with local contractors to fix the pump? What if we used our logistics capability to deliver building materials?

My company commander once told me, not unkindly, that I was "thinking like an NGO." He meant it as a redirect. I took it as a diagnosis.

After separation, I joined a construction technology startup in Austin. The company makes project management software for commercial builders — not exactly saving the world, but I wanted to learn how buildings actually get built before trying to change how they get built. In two years, I went from sales engineer to head of customer success. Our retention rate went from 74% to 91% because I obsessively studied why contractors churned and rebuilt our onboarding around their actual pain points, not our assumptions.

But what matters most to me isn't software and it isn't construction. What matters most to me is shelter — the most basic thing a person needs after food and water, and something that 1.6 billion people worldwide don't have adequately.

I've been volunteering with Habitat for Humanity since I left the Army. Last year, I led a build team in the colonias along the Texas-Mexico border. The families we built for were incredible — resourceful, hardworking, and stuck in a system that makes it nearly impossible to build legal, safe housing. Permitting takes eighteen months. Materials cost three times what they cost fifty miles north. The local building codes were written for suburban development, not for the incremental construction that's actually happening.

I don't want to build houses one at a time. I want to fix the system that makes building houses so hard. That means rethinking building codes, materials supply chains, and financing models for the people who need housing most.

At Stanford, I want to work at the intersection of technology and housing policy. I want to learn from people who've thought about infrastructure at scale — not just in developing countries, but in American communities that look a lot more like the developing world than most people realize.

My platoon sergeant once told me that the best leaders are the ones who can't stop noticing what's broken. I notice broken housing systems everywhere I look. The colonias in Texas. The informal settlements I saw overseas. The mobile home parks three miles from downtown Austin where teachers and paramedics live because they can't afford anything else.

I want to spend my career fixing what I notice. Stanford is where I want to learn how.
strengths: Unique military-to-social-impact arc
Concrete operational achievements (91% retention)
Raw honesty about misery and misfit
coach_notes: This essay breaks the 'military applicant' mold by not leading with heroism but with restlessness. The 'thinking like an NGO' line from the company commander is the kind of detail that can't be manufactured. The colonias example grounds the big vision in something specific and real.

prompt: How do you plan to use the Wharton MBA program to help you achieve your future professional goals?
content:
In 2022, I watched a $340 million private equity deal fall apart because neither side understood the other's numbers. I was the junior associate responsible for the financial model, and I'd built what I thought was a bulletproof DCF. But the target company — a regional hospital network in the Midwest — valued things my model couldn't capture: community trust, physician retention rates tied to a culture of autonomy, and a charity care program that lost money but kept the hospital's tax-exempt status.

The deal died. My managing director blamed the seller's "unrealistic expectations." I think we just didn't speak their language.

That experience set me on a path. For the past three years, I've been building expertise at the intersection of healthcare and finance — first in PE, now in healthcare-focused investment banking. I've closed fourteen transactions totaling $2.1 billion. But each deal has reinforced the same lesson: the gap between financial buyers and healthcare operators is widening, and the consequences land on patients.

My goal is to build an advisory firm that specializes in healthcare transactions — one that can translate between the language of IRR and the language of patient outcomes. Not a traditional bank, but a firm where every analyst has spent time on a hospital floor and every healthcare consultant can read a cap table.

Wharton is where I need to be for three specific reasons.

First, the Health Care Management program. Professor Scott Harrington's work on health insurance markets and Professor Guy David's research on healthcare labor markets address exactly the knowledge gaps I have. I want to understand the economics of care delivery — not just the economics of care delivery companies.

Second, Wharton's emphasis on quantitative rigor. My financial modeling skills are strong, but I need the analytical toolkit to build frameworks that incorporate non-financial value drivers. The Statistics Department's work on causal inference feels directly applicable to measuring how transaction structures affect patient outcomes.

Third — and most importantly — the network. Healthcare transformation requires people who can operate across boundaries. I want to recruit from Wharton's dual-degree students who combine business training with medical or nursing backgrounds. The Health Care Club's annual conference brings together exactly the kind of people I'd want as partners, clients, and collaborators.

I know this goal is ambitious. An advisory firm is not a typical post-MBA job, and I'd plan to spend two to three years at a healthcare-focused consulting firm first, building the operating knowledge I still lack. But I've seen what happens when financial transactions in healthcare are handled by people who don't understand healthcare. My firm will be built by people who do.
strengths: Opens with a vivid, specific failure that frames everything
Names specific professors and research
Realistic about timeline (2-3 years post-MBA)
coach_notes: Wharton wants specificity and this essay delivers. The $340M deal story isn't just color — it's the thesis of the entire essay. Naming professors and specific research areas shows genuine interest. The 'not a typical post-MBA job' acknowledgment builds credibility.

prompt: How do you plan to use the Wharton MBA program to help you achieve your future professional goals?
content:
I grew up in Lagos, where my mother ran a small import business from our living room. She had no formal business training, but she understood something most MBA graduates don't: in emerging markets, relationships are infrastructure. When the banks wouldn't lend to her, she built a network of suppliers who extended credit based on her reputation. When customs officials created bottlenecks, she knew exactly who to call.

Her business plateaued at a level that kept our family comfortable but never grew beyond what one person could manage. The ceiling wasn't talent or market opportunity — it was systems. She had no way to formalize the trust networks that made her business work, no way to train others to replicate her judgment, no way to scale beyond her personal bandwidth.

I left Lagos at eighteen to study economics at the University of Toronto. After graduating, I spent four years in management consulting at a top firm in New York, working primarily on operations transformations for retail and consumer goods companies. I've helped billion-dollar companies redesign their supply chains. Every engagement has taught me something about what my mother was missing: not knowledge, but infrastructure.

My goal is to build a technology platform that helps small and medium enterprises in West Africa formalize and scale their trade operations — inventory management, supplier relationships, trade finance, and logistics — without requiring them to abandon the informal networks that actually work.

At Wharton, I want to combine the MGEC concentration with courses in fintech and entrepreneurship. Professor Gad Allon's work on operations management in emerging markets is directly relevant to my thesis that you can't just transplant Western supply chain models into African markets. The Wharton Global Modular Courses, particularly in emerging economies, would let me test my assumptions on the ground.

I also want to be part of the Wharton Africa Business Forum and the Entrepreneurship Club. I've already connected with two Wharton alumni who built fintech companies in Nigeria — one in payments, one in lending — and both have told me that the Wharton network in Lagos is tight and deeply collaborative.

I know the path from here to there isn't straight. After Wharton, I plan to join a fintech company operating in African markets for two to three years, building the product and operational instincts I'll need before launching my own platform.

Every time I call my mother, she asks me when I'm coming home. I tell her I'm building something. She says, "Build it fast — I'm not getting younger." She's kidding. Mostly.
strengths: Mother's story as origin — specific and unsentimental
Clear gap identification (systems, not talent)
Humor in the closing line feels natural
coach_notes: This essay succeeds because the writer positions their mother not as a victim but as a brilliant operator constrained by systemic gaps. The line 'relationships are infrastructure' does more work than most entire paragraphs. The closing line with the mother is perfect — human, funny, and motivating without being heavy.

prompt: How will a Booth MBA help you achieve your immediate and long-term post-MBA career goals?
content:
Immediately after Booth, I want to join a top-tier venture capital firm focused on enterprise software. Within ten years, I want to be leading investments in companies that are rebuilding critical industry infrastructure — healthcare records, supply chain management, government services — using AI.

I've spent the last four years as a product manager at a B2B SaaS company in Chicago, and before that two years as a software engineer. I understand how enterprise software gets built. What I lack is the financial rigor to evaluate these companies as investments, and the network to get a seat at the table where these decisions are made.

Booth gives me both. The flexible curriculum lets me go deep on venture capital — I'd take courses like Private Equity and Venture Capital with Professor Steven Kaplan, and Entrepreneurial Finance. But I'd also take courses in machine learning and AI applications through the joint offerings with the CS department, because I believe the best investors in AI need to understand the technology at a technical level.

Booth's emphasis on data-driven decision making matches how I think. I'm the person who builds a model before making a recommendation. I want to be surrounded by people who think the same way.

The Chicago tech ecosystem also matters. Unlike most MBA programs, Booth is in a city with a thriving enterprise tech scene — companies like Grubhub, Tempus, and Avant. The access to local startups through the Polsky Center and New Venture Challenge means I can start building my investment thesis while I'm still in school.
strengths: Extremely concise — respects the 250-word limit
Clear immediate + long-term goal structure
Leverages Chicago ecosystem specifically
coach_notes: Booth's essay is short (250 words), so every word needs to earn its place. This writer doesn't waste space on backstory — they state goals, identify gaps, and show how Booth fills them. Naming Kaplan and the Polsky Center shows real research. The 'data-driven decision making' line connects personal style to Booth culture.

prompt: How will a Booth MBA help you achieve your immediate and long-term post-MBA career goals?
content:
My immediate goal is to transition from nonprofit management into social enterprise consulting, helping mission-driven organizations scale without losing their soul. Long-term, I want to launch a fund that invests in social enterprises across Latin America.

For six years, I've run operations for a youth education nonprofit in Chicago's South Side. We grew from serving 200 students to 1,400, and I've managed a $3.2 million annual budget. I know how to do a lot with a little. What I don't know is how to build the financial structures that would let organizations like ours grow sustainably — beyond grant cycles and donor fatigue.

Booth's Social Enterprise Initiative is the primary reason I'm applying. The courses in impact investing and social enterprise management would give me the toolkit I'm missing. Professor John List's work on scaling interventions is literally the question I wrestle with every day: what works at 200 doesn't automatically work at 2,000.

I also want the quantitative foundation Booth is known for. My decisions have been intuition-driven for too long. I want to pair that intuition with analytical rigor — especially in financial modeling and data analysis.

The Booth network in Chicago's social sector is strong. I've already met three alumni working at organizations I admire — one at the Chicago Community Trust, one at IFF, one who launched her own education consultancy. They've all told me the same thing: Booth gave them permission to think about impact and returns in the same sentence.

That's exactly what I need.
strengths: Clear trajectory: nonprofit ops to social enterprise to fund
Specific scale metrics (200 to 1,400 students)
Authentic connection to Chicago community
coach_notes: This essay shows that Booth admits strong nonprofit candidates when they demonstrate analytical potential. The writer doesn't apologize for their background — they frame it as operational proof. The John List reference is perfect for Booth's culture. The closing line from alumni ('permission to think about impact and returns in the same sentence') is memorable.

prompt: Kellogg leaders are primed to tackle challenges everywhere. Describe a time you have demonstrated leadership and created lasting value.
content:
In my third year at a major consumer goods company, I inherited a product line that nobody wanted. Our organic baby food range had been losing share for eight consecutive quarters. The previous brand manager had left. The team was demoralized. My VP told me to "stabilize it" — corporate code for "manage the decline."

I didn't want to manage a decline. I wanted to understand why we were losing.

I spent my first month doing something my predecessors hadn't: I talked to parents. Not focus groups in a sterile conference room — actual conversations at grocery stores, daycare pickups, and online parenting forums. I brought our food scientist with me to a mommy-and-me class in suburban Minneapolis. She was mortified. I was fascinated.

What I learned surprised me. Parents weren't leaving our brand because of taste or price. They were leaving because our packaging felt clinical — like medicine, not food. The competitor winning share had pouches with hand-drawn illustrations and ingredient stories on the back. Parents were choosing the brand that made them feel like good parents, not the brand with the best nutritional profile.

This insight required our team to challenge a core assumption: that our clinical, science-forward branding was an asset. For the baby food category, it was a liability.

I assembled a cross-functional team — design, food science, supply chain, and sales — and pitched a complete package redesign to our division president. She was skeptical. Repackaging costs money and takes shelf space during the transition. I presented the consumer research, the competitor analysis, and a phased rollout plan that minimized risk.

She approved a test market in the Pacific Northwest. Six months later, the redesigned line saw a 23% increase in unit sales. We rolled out nationally in Q3.

But the lasting value wasn't the sales lift. It was the process. I'd inadvertently created a model for consumer-led product development that didn't exist at our company. Our VP started requiring "field immersion" for every brand refresh. The food scientist who came to that mommy-and-me class now runs our consumer insights function. Three other product lines have used the same approach.

The leadership lesson I took from this is that sometimes the most impactful thing you can do is change what your organization pays attention to. We had plenty of data. We had smart people. What we didn't have was a habit of listening to the people actually using our products in their real lives. I built that habit, and it outlasted me.

That's the kind of leader I want to keep becoming at Kellogg — one who creates systems that make everyone better, not just systems that make me look good.
strengths: Shows leadership through influence, not authority
Lasting value is a process change, not just a metric
Kellogg values alignment: teamwork, empathy, listening
coach_notes: This is exactly what Kellogg means by 'lasting value' — not just a sales win, but a new way of working that outlives the individual. The mommy-and-me class detail is specific and humanizing. The essay shows classic Kellogg virtues (collaboration, empathy) without ever using those words.

prompt: Values are what guide you in your life and work. What values are important to you and how have they influenced you?
content:
My parents are both deaf. Growing up, I was their interpreter — at doctor's appointments, parent-teacher conferences, car dealerships, and once, memorably, at a police station when our neighbor's alarm went off and the officers assumed my father had broken in because he didn't respond to verbal commands.

The value that runs deepest in me is one I didn't have a word for until college: advocacy. Not advocacy in the political sense, but in the simplest sense — speaking up for people who aren't being heard. Sometimes literally.

This value has shaped my career more than any strategic plan. After studying communications at Northwestern, I joined a healthcare communications agency because I'd seen firsthand how much information gets lost when systems don't accommodate different ways of communicating. In five years, I've worked on campaigns for pharmaceutical companies, hospital systems, and health insurers.

The work I'm proudest of was a patient education campaign for a diabetes drug. Standard practice was a glossy brochure written at a 10th-grade reading level. I pushed for something different: a suite of materials at a 5th-grade reading level, in twelve languages, with video versions featuring ASL interpretation. My creative director said it would blow the budget. I showed her the data on health literacy and medication adherence and got approval for a pilot.

The pilot outperformed the standard materials by 34% on comprehension tests and 19% on self-reported medication adherence. It's now the template for all patient-facing materials at our agency.

But advocacy also means knowing when to step back. Last year, I hired a project manager who is hard of hearing. During her first month, I caught myself jumping in to "help" during meetings — finishing her sentences, repeating what she said more loudly. She pulled me aside and said, "I appreciate the instinct, but I need you to let me do this myself." She was right. Advocacy without agency isn't advocacy — it's paternalism.

That correction matters to me. It's the difference between leading with humility and leading with a savior complex.

At Kellogg, I want to bring this value into how I lead teams and approach problems. The Kellogg culture of collaboration resonates with me because collaboration at its best is a form of advocacy — making sure every voice in the room is heard, not just the loudest ones. I'm particularly drawn to the KWEST experience and the emphasis on low-ego leadership that Kellogg alumni consistently describe.

My parents taught me that communication isn't about volume. It's about making sure the message gets through. I want to build a career that lives by that principle.
strengths: Unique personal story that's specific, not generic
Shows growth through the 'step back' anecdote
Professional examples with measurable results (34% comprehension lift)
coach_notes: Values essays are hard because they can feel preachy. This one avoids that trap by grounding 'advocacy' in real, specific moments — the police station, the diabetes campaign, the hard-of-hearing colleague. The self-correction moment ('advocacy without agency is paternalism') shows genuine intellectual growth, which Kellogg loves.

prompt: Through your research and/or reflections, what actions have you taken to learn more about Columbia Business School?
content:
My first real encounter with Columbia wasn't at an info session — it was at a jazz club in Harlem. I'd moved to New York three years ago for a media strategy role, and a colleague invited me to a Tuesday night set at Minton's. The bassist turned out to be a CBS second-year. Between sets, he told me about the Tamer Center for Social Enterprise and how he'd used his summer to consult for a music education nonprofit in the Bronx. I remember thinking: that's the kind of MBA experience I want.

Since then, I've been deliberate about learning what makes CBS different. I visited campus twice — once for a class visit in Professor Oded Netzer's data analytics course and once for the Media & Entertainment Conference. Both times, I was struck by how NYC isn't just the setting for CBS — it's the classroom. Students talked about projects with real companies happening in real time, not case studies from five years ago.

I've had coffee with six CBS alumni and current students across media, tech, and entertainment. Three consistent themes emerged: the network is genuinely accessible (one alum called it "dense, not just large"), the cluster system creates real friendships not just professional contacts, and the location advantage compounds over time. An alum who graduated in 2018 told me she still gets introduced to someone new through CBS connections at least once a month.

Professionally, I've been testing my post-MBA thesis against CBS-specific resources. My goal is to build a content monetization platform for independent creators — the infrastructure layer between creative work and revenue. The Eugene Lang Entrepreneurship Center's programs in venture creation and the partnership with Columbia's journalism school would let me develop this idea with access to the creator ecosystem that literally lives in New York.

I also attended the CBS Admissions webinar on the new Columbia Venture Competition and reached out to a current student who won last year's Media & Entertainment track. Her feedback was specific and practical: she recommended I take Professor Miklos Sarvary's media economics course and join the Entertainment & Media Association early to build relationships that feed into summer recruiting.

The jazz bassist at Minton's eventually graduated and launched a music tech startup in Brooklyn. We still text occasionally. Last month he sent me a link to the application and wrote: "You'd fit here." I think he's right.
strengths: Opens with a memorable, unexpected scene
Shows genuine research depth (6 coffee chats, 2 campus visits)
NYC as classroom theme is organic, not forced
coach_notes: Columbia's 'what actions have you taken' prompt rewards demonstrated interest, and this essay delivers. The jazz club opening is distinctive and memorable. Naming Netzer, Sarvary, Tamer Center, Lang Center, and the Columbia Venture Competition shows real research. The callback to the bassist at the end is a nice structural touch.

prompt: What is your immediate post-MBA professional goal?
content:
Immediately after CBS, I want to join a growth-stage fintech company — specifically one building payment or lending infrastructure for underbanked populations in the US — in a product strategy or business development role.

I've spent four years in traditional banking: two as a credit analyst at JPMorgan and two in corporate strategy at Citi. I understand how banks work — and I understand why they structurally underserve communities like the one I grew up in. The branch in my neighborhood in the South Bronx closed when I was twelve. The nearest bank was a forty-minute bus ride away. My mother used check-cashing services that charged 3% fees because she had no alternative.

At CBS, I plan to take fintech-focused courses including Digital Business Strategy with Professor Kinshuk Jerath and explore the intersection of financial inclusion and technology through the Social Enterprise Program. New York's fintech ecosystem — companies like Plaid, Brex, and Ramp — offers unmatched access for recruiting and learning.

This role would build the operational and product skills I need for my long-term goal: launching a financial services platform designed specifically for cash-intensive small businesses in underbanked communities.
strengths: Extremely focused — 200 words, zero waste
Personal story (South Bronx branch closure) grounds the goal
Names specific CBS resources and NYC companies
coach_notes: Columbia's short-answer format demands precision. This writer packs a personal origin story, professional context, specific CBS resources, and a clear post-MBA plan into under 200 words. The South Bronx detail is powerful because it's not played for sympathy — it's evidence that the writer understands the problem firsthand.

prompt: MIT Sloan seeks students whose personal characteristics demonstrate that they will make the most of the incredible opportunities at MIT, both inside and outside the classroom. We are on a quest to find those whose presence will enhance the experience of other students. We seek thoughtful leaders who will drive change in the world. Tell us about a time when you questioned or challenged a belief or idea. What thinking led you to act? What was the outcome?
content:
At my biotech company, the standard operating procedure for clinical trial recruitment was "cast the widest net." More sites, more patients, faster enrollment. Nobody questioned it because the numbers worked — we hit our enrollment targets consistently.

I questioned it because the demographics didn't.

As a clinical operations associate, I noticed that our Phase III trial for a cardiovascular drug had enrolled 2,400 patients across forty-two sites. On paper, enrollment was ahead of schedule. But when I pulled the demographic data, a pattern emerged: 78% of participants were white males over fifty. The drug was intended for a condition that disproportionately affects Black and Hispanic populations, who were represented at 11% and 6% respectively.

I brought this to my director. Her response was honest but discouraging: "Demographics are a regulatory nice-to-have, not a recruitment metric. We need to hit our timeline." She wasn't wrong about the regulatory landscape — FDA guidance on diversity was aspirational, not mandatory. But I thought she was wrong about the science. A drug tested primarily on white men might not work the same way in other populations. We had evidence of this from other cardiovascular drugs where metabolic differences led to different efficacy profiles.

I didn't have authority to change the recruitment strategy. So I did what an MIT person would do — I built a model. Using publicly available demographic data, our site locations, and recruitment funnel metrics, I showed that adding six targeted sites in communities with higher minority populations would increase diversity to over 30% with only a six-week delay in overall enrollment. I also modeled the risk of a post-approval label limitation if demographic data was too narrow, estimating a potential $180M revenue impact.

I presented the analysis to our VP of Clinical Operations. She was initially resistant — the six-week delay meant pushing back a regulatory submission. But the $180M risk number got her attention. She approved three of the six additional sites as a pilot.

The result: minority enrollment increased to 24% (not the 30% I'd modeled, but significantly better than 17%). More importantly, the subgroup analysis revealed a statistically significant difference in optimal dosing for Black patients — a finding that shaped the prescribing information and potentially prevented adverse outcomes.

I learned two things from this experience. First, challenging a belief requires more than moral conviction — it requires a quantitative argument that speaks the language of the people who make decisions. Second, diversity in clinical trials isn't a political position. It's a scientific necessity. The data proved that.

At MIT Sloan, I want to continue developing the analytical toolkit to make these kinds of arguments. I'm drawn to the Healthcare Lab and the opportunity to work on operational problems in healthcare delivery. I want to learn from classmates who share the instinct to look at the data everyone else is ignoring and ask whether the system is actually working for everyone it's supposed to serve.
strengths: Data-driven narrative fits MIT Sloan culture perfectly
Built a model to make the case — shows initiative
Real scientific outcome validates the challenge
coach_notes: This essay is tailor-made for MIT Sloan. The writer challenged a belief not with moral arguments but with a spreadsheet — exactly the kind of 'principled, data-driven leadership' Sloan prizes. The $180M risk model detail is specific and compelling. The outcome (dosing difference discovered) proves the challenge had real scientific value.

prompt: MIT Sloan seeks students whose personal characteristics demonstrate that they will make the most of the incredible opportunities at MIT, both inside and outside the classroom. We are on a quest to find those whose presence will enhance the experience of other students. We seek thoughtful leaders who will drive change in the world. Tell us about a time when you questioned or challenged a belief or idea. What thinking led you to act? What was the outcome?
content:
Everyone at my robotics startup believed we should build our own hardware. We'd raised a $12M Series A specifically to develop a proprietary robot for warehouse automation. Our CTO had designed it. Our investors had funded it. Our pitch deck featured it on every slide.

I was the head of partnerships — not engineering, not product. Technically, hardware strategy wasn't my lane. But after six months of visiting customer warehouses, I'd developed a conviction that our approach was wrong.

The evidence was simple. Every warehouse I visited already had robots — at least two or three different models from existing manufacturers. The problem wasn't hardware. The problem was that these robots couldn't talk to each other or to the warehouse management system. Companies were buying expensive machines that worked in isolation.

I spent three weeks building a competitive analysis. Our proprietary robot would take eighteen months to reach production and cost $45K per unit. Our customers' average robotics budget was already allocated to existing hardware. We were asking them to rip and replace — the hardest sell in enterprise software, except this was enterprise hardware, which is ten times worse.

My alternative: pivot from building robots to building the operating system that makes existing robots work together. Software, not hardware. Platform, not product.

The CTO was furious. The investors were nervous. My CEO asked me to present my analysis to the full leadership team. I did, and the room split. Three people agreed with me. Four didn't. The CTO said I was "oversimplifying the problem."

I didn't win the argument that day. But I asked for permission to run a small experiment: could I take two engineers and build a prototype integration layer using robots already in a customer's warehouse? The CEO gave me eight weeks and a small budget.

In week six, we had a working demo: three different robots from three different manufacturers, coordinated through a single software layer, operating 28% more efficiently than they had independently. The customer's VP of operations watched the demo and immediately asked about pricing.

The company didn't fully pivot — that would have been too dramatic. But we added a software-only product line alongside the hardware. Within a year, the software product accounted for 60% of new revenue. The hardware robot is still in development, behind schedule.

I'm applying to MIT Sloan because this experience showed me what I want my career to be: finding the systems-level solution when everyone else is focused on the component-level problem. Sloan's emphasis on systems thinking — particularly through the System Dynamics group — is exactly the intellectual framework I want to formalize. I want to learn how to see the connections that others miss, and then build businesses around them.
strengths: Classic 'challenge a belief' story executed well
Specific numbers throughout (28% efficiency, 60% revenue)
Shows leadership without authority — influence over hierarchy
coach_notes: This essay is a case study in challenging a belief with data, not ego. The writer knew they were stepping outside their lane and was transparent about it. The 'small experiment' approach is classic entrepreneurial thinking — don't argue for the big pivot, prove it with a small bet. MIT Sloan will love the systems thinking framing.

prompt: Tuck students are nice, smart, and accomplished. Which of these attributes best describes you and why?
content:
I'm going to pick nice, even though my analyst team would probably be surprised to hear it.

Let me explain. In investment banking, being "nice" is not a compliment. It implies softness. When I started as an analyst at a middle-market bank in Charlotte, I was told by a senior VP that my instinct to check on teammates and ask how people were doing would "get trained out of me." It hasn't.

In my four years on the healthcare M&A team, I've built a reputation for two things: rigorous financial modeling and genuinely caring about the people I work with. My managing director calls this "aggressive niceness" — I'm the person who notices when a first-year analyst is struggling at 2am and sits down to walk through the model with them instead of just sending corrections in red. I've mentored six analysts formally and probably a dozen more informally. Three of them have told me directly that my mentorship was why they stayed in banking past their first year.

But nice doesn't mean passive. Last year, our team was working on a sell-side mandate for a rural hospital chain. The buyer's offer undervalued the target by what I estimated to be $40 million based on comparable transactions. My MD was inclined to push the deal through — he'd been working the relationship for two years. I pushed back, respectfully but firmly, with a detailed comparable analysis. We went back to the buyer with a higher ask and closed at $28 million above the original offer.

I pushed back because it was the right thing for our client. But I did it in a way that preserved my relationship with my MD — he later told me he appreciated that I'd come to him privately first instead of raising it in front of the client.

Niceness, as I understand it, isn't about being agreeable. It's about creating the conditions where hard conversations can happen without destroying relationships. In banking, this is rare. At Tuck, from everything I've learned, it's the norm.

I chose Tuck specifically because the community feels like it was designed for people like me — people who believe you can be ambitious and kind at the same time. The small class size, the Hanover setting, the emphasis on collaboration over competition — these aren't features I'd tolerate. They're features I've been looking for.

I want to keep being aggressively nice. Tuck seems like the place where that's not a contradiction.
strengths: Reframes 'nice' as strength without being cheesy
'Aggressive niceness' is memorable and authentic
Concrete examples: mentoring retention, $28M deal improvement
coach_notes: Tuck asks you to pick one attribute, and most applicants agonize over picking 'smart' or 'accomplished.' Picking 'nice' for an investment banker is a bold move that immediately stands out. The essay redefines niceness as a form of courage, which is exactly what Tuck's culture is about. The 'aggressively nice' framing is the kind of phrase admissions committees remember.

prompt: Tell us about your most meaningful collaborative experience and what role you played.
content:
In 2022, a devastating flood hit eastern Kentucky. I was working as a program manager at a large tech company in Seattle, about as far from Appalachia as you can get. But I grew up in Pikeville, Kentucky, and when I saw footage of my hometown underwater, I couldn't just donate and move on.

I organized a disaster relief effort within my company. Not through official channels — our corporate giving team was already running their standard disaster response. I created a grassroots effort focused on something the big relief organizations were missing: technology access. When your home floods, you lose your devices. You can't file FEMA claims, access insurance portals, or communicate with family without a phone or laptop.

I recruited fourteen volunteers from across four teams. We had engineers, program managers, a designer, and two people from legal (essential, because donating company devices had liability implications nobody had considered). I was not the most senior person in the group — our engineering lead was a principal engineer two levels above me. But I was the organizer.

My role was connective tissue. I set up the project structure: what we needed (donated devices, a refurbishment process, a distribution plan), who was responsible for what, and how we'd communicate. I connected our team with the Pikeville public library, which became the distribution hub. I navigated the internal politics to get VP-level approval for donating refurbished devices.

In six weeks, we collected, refurbished, and shipped 340 devices — laptops, tablets, and smartphones — to eastern Kentucky. The library distributed them to families who'd lost everything.

The collaboration was meaningful not because it was efficient — it was messy. Our first two refurbishment batches failed quality checks. The legal review took three weeks longer than expected. One of our volunteers dropped out mid-project for personal reasons and I had to redistribute their work overnight.

It was meaningful because fourteen people who'd never worked together decided that this mattered enough to figure it out. Nobody got promoted for it. Nobody put it on a performance review. We just did it because it needed to be done.

At Tuck, the community emphasis isn't an abstract value — it's what I've been living. When I visited campus, a second-year told me, "Tuck is the kind of place where everyone shows up." That's the culture I want to be part of.
strengths: Personal connection to the crisis grounds the story
Honest about the mess — not a polished hero narrative
Shows organizational leadership, not just participation
coach_notes: This essay nails what Tuck looks for in collaborative experience: genuine initiative, not corporate teamwork. The writer's role as 'connective tissue' is a mature understanding of leadership. The messiness details (failed batches, legal delays, volunteer dropout) make it believable. The Tuck campus visit quote at the end feels earned, not pasted on.

prompt: Michigan Ross is a place where people from all backgrounds with different career goals can thrive. Describe how you would contribute to the diverse Ross community.
content:
I'm a first-generation college graduate who grew up in a trailer park in rural Ohio and now works in management consulting in Chicago. I mention both facts not because either one defines me, but because living in both worlds has given me a specific skill: I can translate between people who don't think they have anything in common.

At my firm, I was staffed on a manufacturing transformation project in Youngstown, Ohio — about an hour from where I grew up. The engagement team was four consultants from East Coast universities who had never been to a factory floor. The client team was plant managers and line supervisors who'd been doing this work for twenty years and resented being told how to do it by kids in Patagonia vests.

I became the bridge. Not because I was the smartest person on the team — I wasn't — but because I understood both languages. I knew that when the plant manager said "that won't work here," he didn't mean he was resistant to change. He meant we hadn't asked him what constraints we were missing. I knew that when my engagement manager said "we need to drive alignment," she meant she needed the plant team to stop rolling their eyes in meetings.

The project succeeded, partly because I spent time translating between the two sides until they could communicate directly. By the end, our engagement manager was having lunch with the plant supervisor and genuinely asking about his daughter's softball season. That wasn't my doing — it's what happens when you break down the assumption that different means difficult.

At Ross, I'd bring this translation ability into the classroom and the community. In action-based learning projects through MAP, I'd push teams to actually listen to their project sponsors — not just extract data, but understand context. In club leadership, I'd advocate for events that bring together students who wouldn't normally intersect — the finance track students and the social impact students, the international students and the Midwest locals.

I'd also bring perspective that's underrepresented in business school: what it's like to grow up without a safety net. I've been on food stamps. I know what it feels like to not understand the unwritten rules that everyone else seems to know. In study groups and case discussions, that perspective is valuable — not because poverty is interesting, but because understanding how most of America actually lives makes for better business decisions.

Ross's culture of 'go blue, go everywhere' resonates because I've already been everywhere — from a trailer park to a consulting firm, and I've learned that the distance between those two places is smaller than most people think.
strengths: First-gen narrative without victim framing
Specific Youngstown example shows the 'translation' skill
Connects personal background to classroom value
coach_notes: This essay takes the diversity prompt and elevates it. Instead of listing demographic checkboxes, the writer shows a specific, practiced skill (translation) that comes from their background. The Youngstown project makes the abstract concrete. The line about 'poverty is interesting' shows self-awareness about how these stories can be received.

prompt: Describe your short-term and long-term career goals. How will the Ross MBA help you achieve those goals?
content:
Short-term: I want to join a climate tech company in a strategy or business development role, specifically one working on decarbonizing heavy industry (steel, cement, chemicals). Long-term: I want to lead the commercial strategy function at a company that's making industrial decarbonization economically viable at scale.

This isn't where I started. I spent three years as a mechanical engineer at a petrochemical plant in Houston. I designed heat exchangers and troubleshot distillation columns. I was good at it and I liked the intellectual puzzle of process optimization. What I didn't like was the growing realization that the systems I was optimizing were part of a problem I cared deeply about.

The turning point was a project where I was asked to evaluate a carbon capture retrofit for one of our units. The technology worked in principle, but the economics were terrible — the retrofit would have cost $140 million and added $23 per ton to our production costs, making us uncompetitive. I watched the project get shelved and realized that climate technology wouldn't win by being morally right. It had to be economically right.

I left engineering and moved into corporate strategy at an industrial conglomerate, where I've spent two years evaluating clean technology investments and partnerships. I now understand the business side of the equation — market sizing, partnership structures, competitive dynamics. What I'm missing is the broader strategic toolkit and the network to move from evaluating climate investments to leading them.

Ross gives me three things I can't get elsewhere. First, the Erb Institute for Global Sustainable Enterprise is unmatched. The dual focus on sustainability and business strategy — not sustainability as a CSR afterthought — is exactly how I think about the problem. I want to take courses that bridge the Ross and SEAS curricula.

Second, Ross's action-based learning model. The MAP program would let me work directly with a climate tech company on a real business problem. I've been in touch with a Ross alum who did her MAP project with a carbon capture startup and said it was the most valuable experience of her MBA.

Third, the Midwest matters. Michigan is at the center of the industrial economy that needs to decarbonize. The auto industry, the manufacturing base, the energy infrastructure — it's all here. Ross's proximity to these industries means access to partnerships, site visits, and career opportunities that coastal programs can't match.

I don't want to work in climate tech because it's trendy. I want to work in it because I've stood inside a petrochemical plant and watched a $140 million solution get shelved because the math didn't work. I want to make the math work.
strengths: Insider credibility — worked in the industry they want to change
$140M project detail makes the problem tangible
Strong 'Why Ross' through Erb Institute and Midwest positioning
coach_notes: This essay works because the writer has credibility from inside the industry. They're not an outsider saying 'industry needs to change' — they're a former plant engineer who saw a solution fail because of economics. The $140M and $23/ton numbers make the problem real. The closing line ('make the math work') is a perfect callback.

prompt: Change: _____ it. In Stern, a changemaker is one who, well, changes things. What is something you have changed, something you would like to change, or something that changed you?
content:
Change: cook it.

I know that sounds like I'm being cute with the prompt. I'm not. Cooking is the most important change agent in my life.

When I moved to New York five years ago for a job in advertising, I was a 23-year-old who could barely boil water. I grew up in a Korean-American household where my mother cooked everything from scratch, and I never learned because I never had to. In Manhattan, I survived on seamless and sadness.

Then my mother got sick. Pancreatic cancer, diagnosed late. She had six months. During those six months, I flew home to Virginia every other weekend and stood in her kitchen while she taught me her recipes. Not from a cookbook — she didn't use one. From memory, from feel. How to know when the doenjang is fermented enough by smell. How to fold mandu so they don't break in the pot. The exact sound a properly seasoned cast iron pan makes when the oil is ready.

She died in March. I cooked her galbi-jjim for the funeral. Forty people told me it tasted just like hers. It didn't — mine was slightly over-salted and the meat was tougher — but I understood what they meant.

Cooking changed me because it taught me that the most important things can't be transmitted through documentation. They require presence, repetition, and the vulnerability of doing something badly in front of someone who loves you.

This lesson reshaped my work. In my advertising career, I moved from strategy into client services because I realized that the best client relationships, like the best recipes, come from showing up consistently and paying attention to the small things. My retention rate across my accounts is 94% over three years — the highest on my team.

At Stern, I want to bring this sensibility to the IQ + EQ culture that defines the school. I'm drawn to the Entertainment, Media, and Technology specialization and the creative industries focus. I plan to start a food-and-culture series through the Korean Business Association — because in my experience, the best conversations about identity, ambition, and belonging happen around a table.

My mother's recipes are in a Google Doc now, as much as they can be. The rest is in my hands. Literally.
strengths: Fills the 'Change: ___' format creatively and authentically
Emotional story that doesn't rely on sentimentality
Links personal experience to professional growth naturally
coach_notes: Stern's 'Change it' prompt is designed to surface personality, and this essay delivers. The cooking framework is unexpected and memorable. The writer doesn't shy away from grief but also doesn't weaponize it — the detail about the galbi-jjim being 'slightly over-salted' is the kind of honesty that can't be faked. The professional pivot (strategy to client services) feels organic, not forced.

prompt: Describe a time when you were part of a community or organization and your unique perspective or talents contributed to the group's success.
content:
When I joined a fintech startup as employee number twenty-three, the engineering team and the sales team didn't talk to each other. Like, literally — they ate lunch at different times, used different Slack channels, and referred to each other as "the other side." I was hired as a product analyst, which meant I sat in between them, both organizationally and physically (my desk was in the hallway between their two areas).

The dysfunction was costing us. Feature requests from customers were getting lost because sales couldn't explain technical constraints to clients, and engineers were building features nobody asked for because they didn't have context on customer needs. Our churn rate was 18% monthly — alarming for a B2B SaaS company.

I started a weekly meeting called "Show and Tell." The name was deliberately childish. Every Thursday, one engineer showed the team what they were building, and one salesperson shared a recent customer conversation. No slides, no formal structure. Coffee and pastries. Ten minutes each, then open discussion.

The first three meetings were awkward. The fourth meeting, an engineer asked a salesperson a genuine question about a customer workflow, and you could feel the energy shift. By month two, people started showing up who hadn't been invited — our CEO, our head of design, an intern.

Within six months, our product development cycle time dropped from fourteen weeks to nine. More importantly, churn decreased to 11%. I can't claim Show and Tell was the sole cause, but our CTO called it "the most impactful process change since we adopted agile."

My contribution wasn't technical brilliance or strategic vision. It was noticing a gap, creating a low-stakes space to bridge it, and being patient enough to let it work. At Stern, I'd bring the same instinct. The IQ + EQ philosophy isn't just a tagline to me — it's how I already work. I notice the spaces between people and I build bridges across them.

Also, I make excellent pastry selections. This matters more than people think.
strengths: Simple, relatable story with measurable impact
Self-awareness — 'my contribution wasn't technical brilliance'
Humor in the closing line feels natural, not forced
coach_notes: This essay is proof that you don't need a dramatic story to write a great essay. 'Show and Tell' is simple, memorable, and shows exactly the kind of community-building instinct Stern values. The metrics (14 weeks to 9, 18% to 11% churn) add credibility. The pastry line at the end is the right amount of personality.

prompt: Describe the biggest commitment you have ever made. Why is this commitment meaningful to you?
content:
In 2020, I committed to representing a group of tenants in a housing discrimination case — pro bono, on my own time, while working sixty-hour weeks as a second-year associate at a corporate law firm.

Let me be clear about what that meant in practice. It meant spending every Saturday for eleven months in the basement of a community center in Bridgeport, Connecticut, interviewing tenants, reviewing lease documents, and building a case file. It meant learning fair housing law from scratch because my firm did corporate M&A, not civil rights litigation. It meant my billable hours dropped, my annual review suffered, and a senior partner told me I was "jeopardizing a promising career for a case that might not go anywhere."

The case went somewhere. Twenty-three tenants in a low-income housing complex had been subjected to discriminatory maintenance practices — units occupied by Black and Latino families had repair requests that took an average of forty-seven days to complete, versus eleven days for white tenants. The property management company denied it. The data was unambiguous.

We filed a complaint with HUD. The case settled in 2022 for $1.2 million in damages and mandatory reforms to the property management company's maintenance protocols. Twenty-three families got checks. More importantly, the settlement included an independent monitor to ensure compliance for five years.

I'm not telling this story because I want credit. I'm telling it because that commitment changed my understanding of what business can and should do. The property management company wasn't some cartoonish villain — it was a private equity-backed platform that managed 8,000 units across three states. The discrimination wasn't conscious; it was systemic. Their maintenance algorithm prioritized units with higher rental income for faster service. In practice, that meant the cheaper units — disproportionately occupied by minority tenants — waited longer.

The fix wasn't a lawsuit. The fix was changing the algorithm. I realized that the tools of business — technology, operations, incentive design — could either create discrimination or eliminate it, depending on who was designing them.

That realization is why I'm leaving law for business school. I don't want to litigate problems after they happen. I want to build systems that prevent them. Yale SOM's mission — educating leaders for business and society — isn't aspirational language to me. It's a precise description of what I'm trying to do.

I'm drawn to SOM's integrated curriculum and the raw/refined case method because both reflect a belief that business problems and social problems aren't separate categories. The Program on Social Enterprise and the collaboration with Yale Law School would let me keep one foot in the legal world while building business skills.

I committed to those twenty-three families. Now I'm committing to making sure the next twenty-three thousand don't need a lawyer at all.
strengths: Demonstrates real sacrifice — billable hours, annual review
The 'algorithm' insight is sophisticated and unexpected
Perfect alignment with Yale SOM's 'business and society' mission
coach_notes: Yale SOM's commitment prompt is designed to surface what you care about enough to sacrifice for. This writer shows the sacrifice explicitly (Saturdays, dropped billings, partner pushback) which makes the commitment credible. The pivot from 'villain narrative' to 'systemic algorithm' shows intellectual sophistication. The closing line is one of the strongest closers in this collection.

prompt: Describe the biggest commitment you have ever made. Why is this commitment meaningful to you?
content:
When I was twenty-five, I moved to a rural district in Zambia for two years to build a teacher training program. I'd been working at a major education nonprofit in DC for three years, mostly doing program evaluation — analyzing data about programs other people built. I wanted to build something myself.

The commitment was straightforward on paper: train eighty primary school teachers in student-centered pedagogy over two years. In practice, it was the hardest thing I've ever done. I arrived speaking basic Bemba (learned from YouTube videos, which is exactly as effective as it sounds). The district had sixty-three schools, most of them one-room buildings with no electricity. My motorcycle broke down so often that the mechanics in Kasama knew me by name.

The first year was mostly failure. My workshops were too theoretical. I was teaching methods designed for schools with textbooks to teachers who shared one textbook among forty students. A veteran teacher named Mrs. Mwamba told me, gently but directly, that I was "very educated and not very useful." She was right.

I rebuilt the program around what actually existed in those classrooms: chalkboards, student notebooks, and the teachers' own knowledge. We developed a peer coaching model where teachers observed each other and gave feedback — no external evaluator needed. By year two, sixty-one of the eighty teachers were actively using at least three new teaching methods. Student performance on the district math assessment improved by 14%.

But the numbers aren't why the commitment was meaningful. It was meaningful because it permanently changed how I see the world. I went to Zambia thinking I was going to help. I left knowing that the teachers I worked with were already brilliant — they didn't need my methods. They needed the space and support to adapt their own.

That shift — from "I have the answer" to "how do I help you find yours" — is why I'm pursuing an MBA at Yale SOM. I want to build an education technology company that amplifies teacher capability rather than replacing it. The edtech industry is obsessed with student-facing products. I want to build teacher-facing products, designed with teachers, for teachers.

Yale SOM's emphasis on cross-sector leadership and the integrated curriculum appeal to me because education operates at the intersection of government, nonprofit, and private enterprise. I need to understand all three. Mrs. Mwamba taught me that being useful requires understanding context before proposing solutions. I want SOM to help me be useful at scale.
strengths: 'Very educated and not very useful' — unforgettable quote
Honest about first-year failure
Clear edtech vision that emerges naturally from the experience
coach_notes: The Mrs. Mwamba quote is the kind of detail that makes an essay memorable. The writer's willingness to share a full year of failure shows maturity. The YouTube Bemba detail is charming without being self-deprecating. The shift from 'I have the answer' to 'how do I help you find yours' is genuinely insightful and connects perfectly to the edtech vision.

prompt: What makes you feel alive when you are doing it, and why? How does it shape how you live your life and/or how you show up at work?
content:
Rock climbing makes me feel alive, but not for the reason people usually assume. It's not the adrenaline. It's the problem-solving.

Every climbing route is a puzzle. You study the wall from below, map a sequence of moves, and then test your plan against reality. Most of the time, reality wins. Your hand can't reach the hold you planned for. Your foot slips. You fall, hang on the rope, study the wall again, and try a different sequence.

I've been climbing for eight years, and the pattern I've noticed is this: the first attempt at any hard route always fails. The second attempt usually fails too. The route goes on the fourth or fifth attempt, when you've accumulated enough information from failures to see the sequence that actually works — the one you couldn't see from the ground.

This is exactly how I approach my work as a product manager at a climate tech startup. When we launched our carbon accounting platform, my first product roadmap was wrong. My second roadmap was less wrong. By the third iteration — after talking to fifty customers, watching them use the product, and documenting every point of confusion — we built something that actually fit their workflow. Our customer activation rate went from 34% to 72%.

Climbing has also taught me something about ego management. On the wall, the route doesn't care about your resume. A 5.12 climb doesn't get easier because you have six years of experience. You either solve the puzzle or you don't. I try to bring that same humility to product decisions — the market doesn't care about how clever my strategy is. It either works or it doesn't.

At Haas, I'm drawn to the "Students Always" defining principle because climbing embodies it. You're never done learning. A route you couldn't do last year becomes possible this year because you've been practicing, failing, and adjusting. That's also how I think about building a company.

I want to bring the climbing mentality into Haas's entrepreneurship ecosystem — through the Skydeck accelerator and the Lean Launchpad course. I'm building a company that will require dozens of iterations before it works. I need to be surrounded by people who understand that the fourth attempt is where things get interesting.

Also, if Haas has a climbing club, I'm in.
strengths: Unexpected metaphor that feels genuine, not contrived
Clean parallel between climbing and product management
Light touch at the end without undermining serious content
coach_notes: Haas's 'what makes you feel alive' prompt invites applicants to show personality, and climbing is a great vehicle because it naturally maps to iteration and humility. The writer doesn't over-explain the metaphor — they trust the reader to see the parallel. The 34% to 72% activation metric grounds it professionally. The closing line is charming.

prompt: The path you are on now has been shaped by a diverse set of experiences, perspectives, skills, and identities. How have the experiences in your life shaped who you are?
content:
I was twelve when my parents told me they were undocumented. I didn't fully understand what it meant until I was sixteen and couldn't get a driver's license. My friends were getting permits and planning road trips. I was learning that some doors are closed before you even know they exist.

My parents came to California from Mexico in 1998. My father works in construction. My mother cleans houses. They are the hardest-working people I know, and for most of my childhood, they lived in constant low-grade fear — of a traffic stop, a workplace inspection, a knock on the door.

I'm a US citizen. I was born here. But growing up in a household shaped by that fear taught me something about systems: they affect everyone in a family, not just the person the policy targets. When my father was afraid to go to the doctor, it was my mother who translated his symptoms for me so I could Google whether it was serious. When DACA passed, I was the one who helped my younger sister fill out the paperwork because my parents' English wasn't strong enough.

I went to UC Berkeley on a full scholarship — the first person in my family to attend college. I studied economics because I wanted to understand the systems that had shaped my family's life. After graduating, I joined a data analytics firm in San Francisco, where I've spent three years building economic models for government agencies. My work on labor market analysis for the California Employment Development Department helped redesign a job matching algorithm that increased placement rates for non-English-speaking workers by 22%.

That project felt personal. It felt like building the tool my father needed twenty years ago.

I'm applying to Haas because I want to build a company that makes government services more accessible to immigrant communities — starting with employment, expanding to housing and healthcare. The "Question the Status Quo" principle resonates because I've been questioning the status quo since I was twelve and wondering why a piece of paper determined my family's worth.

I know this essay is more personal than most applicants write. I share it because I believe business schools need people who understand what it feels like to be on the wrong side of a system. Not as an academic exercise, but as a lived experience. My parents never had the chance to go to business school. I do. I intend to use it to build the systems they deserved.

My father still works in construction. He's sixty-one. When I told him I was applying to Haas, he said, "About time someone in this family got to sit at the table."
strengths: Deeply personal without being self-pitying
Connects family story to professional work seamlessly
Father's closing quote is unforgettable
